heroText: Naypyidaw is a capital city in Myanmar, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

highlights.name: Naypyidaw
highlights.text: Naypyidaw is a capital city in Myanmar, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

history.label: Origins
history.text: Naypyidaw is Burmese for "abode of the king", and is generally translated as "royal capital", "seat of the king", or "abode of kings".
Naypyidaw was founded on a greenfield site near Pyinmana, about 320 kilometres (200 mi) north of the old capital, Yangon.
On 27 March 2006, more than 12,000 troops marched in the new capital in its first public event: a massive military parade to mark Armed Forces Day, the anniversary of Burma's 1945 revolution against.

The nearby town of Pyinmana is a long-standing settlement and had been a pre-colonial governership that dates to the Pagan kingdom era. 2025 earthquake The city suffered extensive damage in the 2025 Myanmar earthquake.

history.label: Growth
history.text: Traditionally, it was used as a suffix to the names of royal capitals, such as Mandalay, which was called Yadanabon Naypyidaw (ရတနာပုံနေပြည်တော်).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The city was officially named Naypyidaw during these ceremonies, and the official, albeit mostly administrative, capital of the country was relocated from Yangon to Naypyidaw. Rationale behind moving the capital There are several assumptions.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Naypyidaw is more centrally located than the old capital, Yangon.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: It is also a transportation hub located adjacent to the Shan, Kayah, and Kayin states.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The official explanation for moving the capital was that Yangon had become too congested and crowded with little room for future expansion of government offices.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Historian Michael Aung-Thwin posits that the relocation to central Myanmar represented a historic return to the Dry Zone, where previous Burmese royal capitals had traditionally been established over a millenia.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The residential areas of Zabuthiri Township and Bawgatheikdi wards, where several apartment buildings collapsed, were especially impacted by the earthquake.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The earthquake prompted Myanmar's military leader, Min Aung Hlaing, to order a redrawing of the city's layout.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Following the earthquake, all government ministry websites and state-run MRTV and Myawaddy online broadcasts went offline.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: In mid-April, a Ministry of Construction assessment found that one-third of government staff buildings in nine of Naypyidaw's wards were rendered uninhabitable or destroyed.
